coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Opuntia sp. extract and their application in phytic acid detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Centeno, Lizeth
Romero-García, Jorge
Alvarado-Canché, Carmen
Gallardo-Vega, Carlos
Télles-Padilla, G.
Díaz Barriga-Castro, Enrique
Cabrera-Álvarez, Edgar N.
Ledezma-Pérez, Antonio
de León, Arxel
coredata.dc:description: Phytic acid and phytate anions are among the principal reserves of phosphorus. A few methods have been used for their detection, but these methods require harsh conditions. In this work, graphene quantum dots are successfully synthesized by Opuntia sp. extract through a pyrolysis microwave treatment. This green synthesis method allows us to controllably obtain nanostructures with excellent dispersion and a narrow size distribution to use for detecting phosphates and phytic acid and amplifying fluorescence quenching at pH = 8.8. Their application allows for a one-step turn-off system. The quenching percentage for phytic acid (PA) detection is 53%, and the quenching percentages are 26 and 21% for sodium phosphate monobasic (SPMB) and sodium phosphate dibasic (SPDB), respectively. The Stern-Volmer analysis shows that static and dynamic quenching mechanics contribute to phytic acid quenching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Optical sensors
Phytic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Opuntia sp.
Detection of phytic acid and phosphates
Synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Opuntia sp. extracts
Analysis of the detection of phytic acid and phosphates by GQDTs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological analysis of graphene quantum dots: a) TEM image, b) HRTEM image and c) histogram of the particle size distribution.
a) EDX spectra of graphene quantum dots and b) EDX data.
FTIR spectrum of the GQDTs obtained from the nopal extract.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the GQDTs (black square-line) and emission spectra (red circle-line), b) optical micrograph of the GQDTs, and c) fluorescence micrograph of the GQDTs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a, b and c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the GQDTs at different concentrations, ranging from 0, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 mM (PDB, PM and PA, respectively) and the d) effect of phosphate concentration on the fluorescence intensity.

a, b and c) Stern-Volmer plots of the GQDTs at different concentrations, ranging from 0 to 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 mM (SPDB, SPMB, and PA as quenchers, respectively), d) quenching percentage at 500 mM and the insert is the e) absorbance spectra of PA, SPMB, and SPDB with GQDT.
Effect of fluorescence intensity in real samples (rolled oats and pinto beans).
Illustration showing the synthesis of graphene quantum dots and phytic acid detection.
Phytic acid concentration in real samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Opuntia sp. extract and their application in phytic acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigación en Química Aplicada, Boulevard Enrique Reyna 140, 25294 Saltillo, Coahuila, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Química Aplicada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CONACYT-Centro de Investigación en Química Aplicada

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CONACYT-Centro de Investigación en Química Aplicada, Boulevard Enrique Reyna 140, 25294 Saltillo Coahuila, México.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phytic acid and phytate anions are among the principal reserves of phosphorus. A few methods have been used for their detection, but these methods require harsh conditions. In this work, graphene quantum dots are successfully synthesized by Opuntia sp. extract through a pyrolysis microwave treatment. This green synthesis method allows us to controllably obtain nanostructures with excellent dispersion and a narrow size distribution to use for detecting phosphates and phytic acid and amplifying fluorescence quenching at pH = 8.8. Their application allows for a one-step turn-off system. The quenching percentage for phytic acid (PA) detection is 53%, and the quenching percentages are 26 and 21% for sodium phosphate monobasic (SPMB) and sodium phosphate dibasic (SPDB), respectively. The Stern-Volmer analysis shows that static and dynamic quenching mechanics contribute to phytic acid quenching.

ce:section.ce:para: Phytic acid (myo-inositol 1,2,3,4,5,6-hexakis dihydrogen phosphate) (Fig. 1 ) and phytate anions are a principal reserve of phosphorus in most commercial agricultural grains and some legume plants [1]. Generally, these phosphorus reserves are not bioavailable to nonruminant animals because they lack the enzyme (phytase) required for their hydrolysis [2]. When nonruminant livestock is fed grains, such as swine, fowl, and fish, the unabsorbed phytate passes to the digestive tract. Phytate is then excreted to manure, elevating the amount of phosphorus and leading to environmental problems, mainly eutrophication [3]. PA also chelates minerals, iron, zinc, and calcium, forming insoluble complexes in the gastrointestinal tract. It works as an antinutrient factor in human and monogastric domestic animal diets [4]. Nevertheless, phytic acid (PA) and phytate can potentially be used in industrial applications. Li et al. found that the chelation complex formed by PA, melamine, and Mn2+ ions, which is called a melamine phytate nanosheet, has been successfully utilized with ammonium polyphosphate. This complex, blended with polypropylene, can replace an intumescent flame retardant [5]. Want et al. reported biobased PA as an excellent double-crosslinked element in preparing aerogels prepared with montmorillonite and poly(vinyl alcohol). This results in materials with ultrahigh mechanical properties and thermal insulation in extreme environments [6]. Other applications for the use of PA are documented, including a biobased clay-protein adhesive [7], a precursor for the preparation of a chemical catalyst of methanation [8], and PA assembled with polyaniline through boronate bonds, which has been suggested as a hydrogel sensor for the electrochemical detection of microRNA [9]. A few methods have been used to determine the content of PA and phytate anions in plant tissues and manure. In general, these methods are classified into specific and nonspecific analytical methods. In the nonspecific analysis classification, the “ferric precipitation” [10] and “Haug and Lantzsch” methods [11] are included, where the total inositol is measured. Such analytical determinations of PA have severe disadvantages. They require the use of harsh conditions. Furthermore, there are interferences of reactive phosphorus, also known as dissolved orthophosphate phosphorus (P-O-P), nonreactive phosphorus (including P-O-P bonds), and dissolved organic phosphorus (DOP) (P-O-C bonds) [12]. Specific analytical techniques include 31P nuclear magnetic resonance (13P-NMR) spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) [13]. In the last decade, the use of biosensors has merged as an alternative for the detection of phytic acid. Many of these biosensors are based on the use of nanomaterials and nanostructures, such as nanoparticles, nanofibers, and quantum dots.

Quantum dots are a fascinating set of materials [14]. They are materials that exhibit interesting fluorescence properties. The ability to efficiently fluoresce is the main reason why quantum dots have gained so much potential for many sensing applications [15]. Most quantum dots currently being produced are made of semiconducting inorganic materials, such as cadmium selenide (CdSe), but graphene has emerged as a possible alternative. Graphene quantum dots (GQDTs) are not only being trialed in academic laboratories but also are being made commercially by a select number of companies [16]. To date, graphene quantum dots have the potential to be used in packaging [17], solar cells [18], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [19], electronic displays [17], antibacterial and disinfection systems [20], release systems [21], batteries, supercapacitors [22], flash memory devices [23], photocatalysis [24], various optical sensors and biosensors [25], bioimaging [26] and theranostic applications [27]. Like many graphene products (and structural forms), graphene quantum dots can be made up of single-layer or few-layer graphene, with the most common being quantum dots utilizing few-layer graphene. At this scale, the graphene sheets exhibit different properties from other single sheets with a larger lateral dimension/flake size. These properties include specific optical, electronic (and optoelectronic), spin and photoelectric properties, as well as the ability to fluoresce and emit light in the blue range of the electromagnetic spectrum. All of these properties correlate to other properties, such as biocompatibility, inertness, and low toxicity. There are several reports about their application in optical sensors, such as turn-off and turn-on systems for the detection of phosphates [28]; glyphosate [25]; inorganic ions [29]; organic materials, such as H2O2 [30]; and bisphenol-A [31].

Many different methods can be used to create graphene quantum dots. Early efforts focused on using graphene as the starting material [32]. Other methods have been deemed too expensive, such as electron beam lithography (EBL), laser ablation, and various electrochemical synthetic routes [33]; thus, these are not ideal choices for large­scale production and use. One method that has arisen in recent years is to use other carbon-based materials, such as coal, as the starting material in an environmentally clean synthesis [34]. Additionally, the green synthesis of nanostructures using plant extracts has been explored. Green syntheses allow ecofriendly, easily scalable, and nontoxic GQDTs to be obtained from low-cost raw materials and the elimination of chemical agents during synthesis [35]. Nopal (Opuntia sp.) is a native plant in Mexican arid zones and is widely used for glucose, cholesterol, and triglyceride treatment. Because of its chemical composition, the extract of this plant has high potential to be explored for synthesizing GQDTs [36].

In this paper, we report the synthesis of graphene quantum dots from ethanolic Opuntia sp. extracts by one-step microwave pyrolysis and their application in sodium phosphate monobasic (SPMB), dibasic (SPDB), and phytic acid sodium salt (PA) detection; in this case, used as quenchers. These GQDTs can eliminate the use of inorganic ions in turn off and turn-on systems for fluorescence sensing mechanisms. Results show a phenomenon known as amplified fluorescence quenching attributed to J-aggregate formation by electrostatic interactions between the GQDTs and phosphates or direct structure of (PO 4 3−) ions, which are high proton acceptors. The quenching percentage for phytic acid reaches 53%, and for SPMB and SPDB, is the quenching percentages are nearly 26 and 21%, respectively.

Opuntia sp., pinto beans, oats and rice were purchased from a local supermarket. Ethylene glycol (99.8%) isopropyl alcohol (99.9%), and sodium phosphate monobasic (98%) were supplied by JT Baker. Phytic acid sodium salt hydrate (98%), sodium hydroxide pellets (99%),32-mm dialysis tubing, ethanol (99.9%), and sodium phosphate dibasic (99%) were acquired from Aldrich Chemical Company.

The extraction was carried out with fresh cladodes of Opuntia sp.; the cladode was washed with distilled water to remove dust and foreign materials. The material was cut into approximately 1 cm3 cubes. A glass beaker containing 400 ml of ethanol was heated for 5 min. The chopped cladodes were incorporated in this glass beaker and magnetically stirred at 80 °C for 10 min. The material remains were left to rest for 2 h. Finally, they were filtered with a funnel using sterile gauze cheese cloth. The excess ethanol was removed with the help of a Multi-Vapor E-16 instrument. The powders were stored in a tightly closed bottle and frozen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Opuntia sp.
ce:section.ce:para: In a one-neck flask, 500 mg of Opuntia sp. extract was dispersed in a mixture of 10 ml of ethylene glycol and 2.5 ml of ultrapure water and magnetically stirred for 24 h. This suspension was transferred to a microwave oven (MARS 6 SYNTHESIS, MATTHEWS.NC) operating at a power of 600 W for 4 min. Then, the product was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and dialyzed for 24 h at a neutral pH using dialysis tubing (benzoylated, 32 mm Ꝋ), washed with ethanol and acetone and dried under ambient conditions. The product of this reaction are the GQDTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of phytic acid and phosphates
ce:section.ce:para: We prepared 0.01 M solutions of phytic acid, sodium phosphate monobasic, and sodium phosphate dibasic dissolved in 1 M sodium hydroxide; these solutions were adjusted to pH 8.8. A 1% diluted suspension of GQDTs in double-distilled water was prepared. Briefly, 200 μl of the GQDTs suspension was mixed in Eppendorf tubes with appropriate volumes of the PA, SPDB, and SPMB solutions to reach solution concentrations of 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 mM. After reaching a total volume of 1 ml, the tubes were left to rest for 30 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of each sample were acquired at an excitation wavelength of 250 nm and a pH of 7.

Real sample detection using pinto beans (Phaseolus vulgaris) and rolled oats (Avena sativa) was carried out with a modified version of a previously reported procedure [11,37], and 0.10 g of the flour samples were extracted with 20 ml of 0.65 N HCl for 24 h and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was centrifuged again at pH 8.8 and 12,000 rpm. At this point, 200 μl of the supernatant was removed and 200 μl of GQDTs was added. Then, the volume was brought up to 1 ml, and the mixture was left to rest for 30 min. Fluorescence spectra were acquired at an excitation wavelength of 250 nm and a pH of 7.

The graphene quantum dot morphology was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an FEI Titan microscope at 300 kV. The samples were prepared by the dropwise addition of the GQDT dispersion (in isopropyl alcohol, 0.1 mg/ml) on lacey carbon and lacey silicon grids. ATR Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was carried out with a Nicolet iS5 Thermo Scientific iD7ATR instrument using the following parameters: 4000–600 cm−1, 16 resolutions, 64 scans, and germanium as the reference standard material. The fluorescence images were recorded on an Olympus cx41 fluorescence microscope with a 300 nm filter by casting a few drops of the dispersed GQDT solution (in isopropyl alcohol, 1 mg/ml) on a glass slide substrate. The light dispersion was determined by a Nanotrac Wave instrument, and the zeta potential was obtained by a Microtrac Zeta Check instrument. The absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu 2401PC spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence spectra were obtained with a Biotek SYNERGY H1 microplate reader and Avaspec ULS2048CL-EVO spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots from Opuntia sp. extracts
ce:section.ce:para: After the microwave oven pyrolysis process, the color of the Opuntia sp. extract suspension changes from green to brown, which indicates the formation of graphene quantum dots. GQDT formation occurs when carbon materials absorb microwave radiation and transform into heat; this process is called dielectric heating [38]. After that, pyrolytic compounds decompose and then self-assemble into nanosheets, as previously reported [39].

The nanostructures are stable with a zeta potential of 33 mV, and the product is considered a stable suspension, allowing manipulation for several months. The result of light dispersion shows an MN size of 65 nm. Scheme 1 is a representative image of the synthesis process and phytic acid detection with GQDTs.

The morphological analysis obtained by TEM shows an excellent dispersion of nanostructures (Fig. 2 ) without aggregates, and the quasi-spherical morphology has an average size of 2.6 ± 0.63 nm; this result shows the narrow distribution of the nanoparticles. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrograph shows that the lattice spacing of 0.21 ± 0.002 corresponds to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon, as previously reported [40], and interstitial defects may be a result of the thermal degradation process to which it was subjected.

An elemental analysis was carried out on lacey silicon and showed that the GQDTs were composed of carbon (77%) and oxygen (18%), which were attributed to functional groups, such as CO and C-O-C. The presence of Mg, S, Cl, K, and Ca were attributed to impurities deriving from the dialysis process or the subproducts of the Opuntia sp. extract (Fig. 3 ).

The ATR-FTIR vibrational spectroscopy analysis results (Fig. 4 ) show that the infrared spectrum of the graphene quantum dots is quite similar to that found in the literature29b [41]. The peak in the region of 3688–3000 cm−1 is the OH stretching band, indicating the presence of OH or carboxyl groups. The CH stretching band at 2946–2878 cm−1 is attributed to CH bonding to the conjugated skeleton of the GQDTs. The absorption band at 1648 cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching of the carboxylic group. The signals from 1440 to 1395 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching, and the peaks at 970–827 cm−1 are assigned to the CC vibrations of the epoxy group. The peak at 1200 cm−1 shows the symmetric stretching of C-O-C, and the band at 1039 cm−1 corresponds to CO.

The absorption spectra show a peak with one maximum at 263 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC in the aromatic ring for the graphene backbone [42]. The emission spectra extend from approximately 300 nm to 400 nm and have a maximum at 334 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 253 nm. The excited-state relaxation shifts to the basal state corresponding to the π* → π transition (Fig. 5 ) [43].

The image obtained by fluorescence microscopy shows a large particle attributed to the agglomeration of nanoparticles. Under normal optical microscopy conditions, emission is not observed (Fig. 5b). In contrast, the micrograph for fluorescence microscopy confirms the green emission of nanoparticles synthesized from the Opuntia sp. extract (Fig. 5c), and the presence of large nanoparticles is attributed to the agglomerations that form during sample preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the detection of phytic acid and phosphates by GQDTs
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of dibasic phosphate and monobasic phosphate show a linear behavior with R2 values of 0.98 and 0.85, respectively. While phytic acid shows a quadratic function with an R2 of 0.91, its asymptotic behavior can be appreciated with a PA limit of detection of 500 mM (Fig. 6 ).

There are reports about phosphate detection using graphene quantum dots [44]. The difference lies in the use of Fe(III) as a quencher and emission recovery by incorporating phosphates to promote a complex between phosphates and Fe(III). These works, by analyzing the pH behavior and indicating a decrease in emission recovery at alkaline pH values, report a better response to Fe(III) at pH 5.5 [28,45].

Therefore, phosphate detection was carried out at pH 8.8 and PA, SPMB, and SPDB functioned as GQDT quenchers. There are reports where sodium phosphate and other phosphate ions can realize fluorophore quenching, favoring aggregates for amplified quenching [46].

Notably, (PO 4 3−) ions, which are high proton acceptors, are directly formed [47]. In this case, these ions can promote aggregation. Thus, electrostatic interactions exhibit a phenomenon known as amplified fluorescence quenching. It has been reported that when using GQDTs for the detection of trypsin [48], the proximity between GQDTs results in photon reabsorption and no radiative energy transfer [49], thereby the quenching by PA, SPDB and SPMB is promoted. Phytic acid favors aggregation, and the large number of ions (PO 4 3−) at pH = 8.8 increases the quenching effect with respect to the other phosphates (SPMB and SPDB).

The quenching percentage for phytic acid reaches 53%. In contrast, the quenching percentages of SPMB and SPDB are nearly 26 and 21%, respectively (Fig. 7d). This result could be attributed to the lest number from aggregates form by a diminution of electrostatics interaction, and fewer (PO 4 3−) ions that limit the energy transference. The absorbance spectra show overlap of the GQDTs and SPMB and SPDB with the GQDTs, while PA shows a small shoulder attributed to the presence of J-aggregates increasing the quenching percentage with respect to that of SPMB and SPDB.

The quenching is described by the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 1) [50].
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k sv
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities with and without a quencher, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Q] is the quencher concentration.

The analysis obtained by the Stern-Volmer equation shows that the quenching constants Ksv for SPDB and SPMB are very close (Fig. 7 and Table 1 ); this result is attributed to their similar chemical structures, which limits aggregation and the number of (PO 4 3−) ions. Both show a linear behavior that can be attributed to dynamic or static quenching. Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer plot for PA shows a curve instead of a line; this result is attributed to the combination of both quenching mechanisms (dynamic and static). Additionally, the process by which more than one fluorophore is acting can indicate why one part has no access to the quencher and does not form aggregates, while the other does [51]. Therefore, the quenching mechanism occurs due to aggregation, which can assist in arranging the electron transport or energy transfer between the GQDTs and ions and limits light emission [52].

The phytic acid content reported in bean literature is 1–1.5% depending on variety, and the PA content obtained for this procedure is 1.15% [37]. The oats are 0.79% (Table 2), and the values reported for oats range from 0.4–1.16% [53], indicating that the proposed method is a good alternative for determining PA in real samples.

The FTIR spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and TEM results reveal graphene quantum dots were synthesized from the Opuntia sp. extract, which is an inexpensive raw material. With its excellent dispersion and narrow size distribution of nanostructures, this prepared material can promote phosphate detection by amplified fluorescence quenching and the formation of (PO 4 3−) ions, which are high proton acceptors at pH = 8.8; this method requires fewer steps than other methods previously reported. Phytic acid can reach up to 53% quenching compared to potassium phosphate monobasic and potassium phosphate dibasic with quenching values of 26 and 21%, respectively; finally, the Stern-Volmer plot shows that the quenching caused by phytic acid can be attributed to both static and dynamic quenching mechanics.
Lizeth Centeno: Methodology.
Jorge Romero-García: Visualization, Investigation.
Carmen Alvarado-Canché: Methodology,
G. Télles-Padilla1 Investigation.
Carlos Gallardo-Vega1 Formal analysis,
Enrique Díaz Barriga-Castro1, Edgar N. Cabrera-Álvarez2: Supervision and visualization.
Antonio Ledezma-Pérez1⁎ and Arxel de León2⁎: Project administration, Writing- Original draft preparation Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of the CONACYT through 303383 (PENTA), 292399 (FORDECYT), and the support of the CIQA through the 6504 internal projects is greatly appreciated. Lizeth Centeno thanks the CIQA (Mexico) for the fellowship.
